text: This is a case of a twenty-one year old male with a chronically incarcerated molgagny hernia. This was causing chronic cough, dyspnea, and chest pain.
CT images were obtained before the surgery showing a very large omental containing molgagny hernia.
The surgeon begins a transabdominal preperitoneal robotic repair of the molgagny hernia, utilizing robotic instruments to access and manipulate the hernia site within the abdominal cavity.

Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon observes the entire omentum incarcerated within the hernia defect and proceeds with the reduction of the omentum, which takes approximately fifteen to twenty minutes.
After reducing the omentum, the surgeon initiates a pre-pectineal dissection, visibly dividing the falciform ligament.
I was somewhat worried that I may not be able to perform a full reduction of the hernia contents and hernia sac. Surprisingly, this came out easier than I had expected.
The surgeon successfully reduces the hernia sac completely, removing it from the hernia defect.
The surgeon performs a circumferential pre-peritoneal dissection after fully reducing the hernia sac, preparing the area for optimal mesh placement.
The surgeon encounters a challenging dissection through the preperitoneal plane, which appears more difficult to navigate compared to a typical ventral preperitoneal plane.
The surgeon observes that the peritoneum is more adherent to the diaphragm compared to a typical ventral hernia, indicating a challenging dissection area.
The surgeon dissects the preperitoneal plane, revealing a large amount of fat on the left side, which is reduced from the hernia site.
The surgeon performs a larger dissection due to bilateral defects, expanding the preperitoneal plane to accommodate mesh placement.
The surgeon continues the preperitoneal dissection, which is visually observed to be tedious and progressing slowly.
After completing the preperitoneal dissection, the surgeon begins closing the hernia defects using a running absorbable OV-lock suture, ensuring adequate mesh overlap.
I think this technique is advantageous for this type of hernia due to the ability to sequentially cinch down this closure.
The pneumoperitoneum is decreased to reduce tension on the hernia closure. An additional OV-lock suture is run from the opposite direction to reinforce the initial closure.
Unfortunately, the video recording stopped here, but this was the majority of the procedure. You can see the final pictures of the closure afterwards.

The surgeon demonstrates the defect closure using a barred soft preperitoneal macroporous polypropylene mesh. The mesh is positioned to exclude it from the peritoneal cavity, followed by a peritoneal closure.
